Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty. Flavor: sweet, rich. Best prep: dry or cure first, then crack, roast, grind, or press as needed. Cautions: northern-hardy cultivars are best in cooler zone 7 sites.
Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: classic pine-nut flavor. Best prep: dry or cure first, then crack, roast, grind, or press as needed. Cautions: best in drier sites.

Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: nutty and starchy after leaching. Best prep: leach tannins first, then roast, grind, or cook. Cautions: tannins require leaching; focus on lower-tannin white-oak-group species.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: oil is mild and buttery. Best prep: dry or cure first, then crack, roast, grind, or press as needed. Cautions: best in protected acidic sites.
Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: starchy rhizome; nutty seeds. Best prep: usually best cooked, roasted, or simmered until tender.
Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: potato-like. Best prep: usually best cooked, roasted, or simmered until tender.

Harvest window: Spring shoots; summer pollen; fall rhizomes
Notes: Status: wild native. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: mild shoots; starchy rhizomes. Best prep: shoots are easiest; rhizomes are best dried, pounded, and washed for starch. Cautions: spreads strongly.
Status: wild native. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: mild seeds. Best prep: best young; use fresh, lightly cooked, or blanched depending on texture.
Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: starchy rhizome; nutty seeds. Best prep: usually best cooked, roasted, or simmered until tender.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: celery-parsley flavor. Best prep: best young; use fresh, lightly cooked, or blanched depending on texture. Cautions: spreads readily in wet ground.

Water: Running water or very moist soil
Notes: Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty. Flavor: peppery. Best prep: best young; use fresh, lightly cooked, or blanched depending on texture. Cautions: use clean water sources only.

Status: wild native. Tastiness: mainly a survival food or only marginally palatable. Flavor: starchy and nutty when processed. Best prep: dry, roast, or otherwise process seeds/rhizomes before eating. Cautions: usually requires drying, roasting, or other processing.
Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: corn-like grain note. Best prep: harvest dry seed, clean, then grind or cook.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: mild nutty grain flavor. Best prep: harvest dry, thresh, winnow, then cook or mill. Notes: Emerging perennial grain crop.

Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: sunflower-seed flavor. Best prep: harvest dry, then clean, thresh, or grind before use. Cautions: tall and spreading.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: buckwheat-like. Best prep: cook or grind seeds; young leaves are milder. Cautions: not as common or proven as annual buckwheat.

Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: oily seed flavor. Best prep: treat like an oilseed or roast lightly. Notes: Breeding target for perennial oilseed production.

Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: tart fruit; green-bean-like pads. Best prep: de-spine pads and fruit carefully; grill, saute, or use fruit fresh. Cautions: remove spines and glochids carefully.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild pads; sweet-tart fruit. Best prep: best fully ripe; fresh, dried, or preserved. Cautions: remove spines and glochids carefully.

Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: salty and earthy. Best prep: use sparingly as a salty green or grind seeds. Cautions: best used in moderation.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: salty, spinach-like. Best prep: best as a salt-seasoned green in mixed dishes. Cautions: best in dry, well-drained sites.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty. Flavor: sweet when roasted. Best prep: slow-roast the heart or flower stalk. Cautions: harvesting the heart kills the plant; sharp spines.

Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild and slightly sweet flowers. Best prep: flowers are best fresh or lightly cooked; immature pods cook better than mature ones. Cautions: older mature pods are fibrous.
